---
title: "Catalogue and Planning"
subtitle: "Inspect what is available and what will run before extraction begins."
sidebar: concepts
---

## Three calls, three different jobs

The high-level workflow separates discovery, planning, and execution.

| Call | Question it answers | Does feature computation run? |
|---|---|---:|
| `available_features()` | What feature IDs can I ask for? | No |
| `plan_features()` | How would my requested features be produced from this input? | No |
| `extract_features()` | Produce the planned outputs. | Yes |

This separation is useful when an extractor is slow, costly, or depends on an
optional model. You can inspect the route and parameters before any model is
loaded or any media is processed.

## The catalogue is the menu

Each catalogue row describes one requestable feature ID. It states what input
kind the feature accepts, what object type it returns, what dependencies it
needs, its default parameters, and whether it requires explicit opt-in.

```{python}
from natural_features.workflows import available_features

text_entries = available_features(modality="text")
[
    {
        "feature_id": entry.feature_id,
        "returns": entry.output_schema,
        "requires_opt_in": entry.requires_opt_in,
    }
    for entry in text_entries
]
```

By default, this call shows public feature IDs: the stable names intended for
users. `public_only=False` also includes internal names used by older recipes,
lower-level tests, and implementation details. Most users should start with
the public list.

## A plan is the filled-in order

A plan combines a requested feature with the actual input you supplied. It
chooses the registered extractor—the function or model that computes the
feature—connects it to a compatible input, merges its default parameters with
your overrides, and records the expected output type.

```{python}
from natural_features.workflows import plan_features

plan = plan_features(
    "The scene opens.",
    features=["text.tokenize"],
)

[
    {
        "requested": row.feature_id,
        "step_id": row.step_id,
        "extractor": row.use,
        "input": row.input_token,
        "returns": row.output_schema,
    }
    for row in plan.rows
]
```

In this example, `input:text` means “pass the text input to this step.” It is a
connection in the plan, not a file path or a second copy of the text.

`FeaturePlan.to_recipe()` converts the plan into the dictionary consumed by
the recipe executor. Nothing runs during that conversion.

## How path and multimodal inputs are understood

Known file suffixes are associated with image, audio, video, text, or NumPy
inputs. A multimodal movie can expose both video and audio routes. Planning
still checks that every requested feature can be reached from the modalities
actually available; a video-only input cannot satisfy an audio-only extractor
unless an earlier step explicitly produces audio.

## Why a request may be rejected during planning

An unknown feature name has no registered route. A modality mismatch means the
requested method needs a different kind of input—for example, an audio method
was given only an image. Optional, costly, or placeholder routes require the
corresponding explicit opt-in. These are planning errors, so they occur before
extraction starts.

Planning does not prove that an optional Python package, model file, API
credential, or system program is installed. The budget controls which routes
may enter a plan; the [Execution modes](execution-modes.qmd) page explains what
happens if a chosen backend is unavailable when the plan runs.

## See also

[List and plan features](../start/02-list-and-plan-features.qmd) · [Feature catalogue](feature-catalogue.qmd) · [Extract features](../start/03-extract-features.qmd)
